ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper introduces a clear new perspective by "neuralizing" the classical entity grid with a convolutional architecture and distributed entity encodings, which is a notable departure from prior hand-crafted grid and graph-based methods. The use of convolutional feature learning to capture long-range entity transitions and an end-to-end pairwise ranking training objective distinguishes the technique from earlier grid and graph models. Given the established nature of the coherence problem, the contribution is a meaningful methodological advance rather than a wholly new problem formulation, so it is notable but not radically transformative.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Yes — the paper addresses a core NLP/discourse problem (local coherence) by neuralizing the entity grid with a convolutional architecture and pairwise ranking, which is squarely within top NLP/ML venues. It uses standard datasets (WSJ, DUC'03), established coherence tasks (discrimination, insertion, summary coherence rating), and appropriate baselines, and reports hyperparameters and comparisons expected by the community. Despite noted limitations (smaller gains on insertion and limited Extended Grid features), the work is well positioned for the venue’s audience and evaluation standards.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claims (neuralizing the entity grid with a convolutional architecture, end-to-end pairwise ranking, and improved performance) are supported by experiments on WSJ and DUC'03 showing state-of-the-art results against reasonable baselines (random, graph-based, grid variants). Mathematical components appear appropriate for a neural model (no proofs claimed) and the experimental setup uses established tasks (discrimination, insertion, summary rating) and baselines. Minor technical concerns remain: the model was trained only on the discrimination task which likely explains smaller gains on insertion, the Extended Grid-CNN used only a subset of features (excluding those needing external corpora), and no mention of statistical significance testing or released code limits reproducibility. Overall these are implementation/training limitations rather than fundamental flaws, so the paper is technically sound with minor issues.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and relevant baselines (Random; Guinaudeau & Strube 2013 graph model; Elsner & Charniak grid variants) using the same datasets (WSJ, DUC'03) and the standard coherence metrics (discrimination, insertion, summary rating). Comparisons appear fair and reported under the same evaluation conditions, and the authors acknowledge limitations (smaller gains on insertion and that training on the discrimination task may not be optimal for insertion). A minor caveat is that the Extended Grid-CNN experiments only include a subset of Extended Grid features (excluding those requiring external corpora), which slightly limits completeness of the comparison.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear progression from motivation through method to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions (neuralizing the entity grid with a convolutional architecture and pairwise ranking training) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and the experiments (discrimination, insertion, summary coherence rating) and baselines are presented clearly, suggesting figures/tables and examples are effective and informative. Writing quality is good overall, though the discussion of smaller gains on the insertion task indicates there could be a bit more targeted exposition or analysis for that harder case.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of local coherence by neuralizing the entity grid with a convolutional architecture that captures long-range entity transitions and incorporates entity-specific distributed representations, trained end-to-end with a pairwise ranking objective. It reports state-of-the-art results on discrimination, insertion, and summary coherence rating tasks (WSJ and DUC'03), which is likely to shift researchers toward neural, end-to-end coherence models. However, gains are smaller on the harder insertion task and the model was trained primarily on discrimination (and some extended-grid features requiring external corpora were excluded), so the contribution is strong and influential but not fully transformative.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Experiments are fairly comprehensive: the model is evaluated on three coherence tasks (discrimination, insertion, summary rating) using WSJ and DUC'03 and compared to several strong baselines, supporting the main claims. Ablation/sensitivity analysis appears limited — Extended Grid-CNN experiments only include a subset of entity-specific features and there is no explicit mention of broader ablations or training-task sensitivity analyses. Related work is reported as thorough and several limitations are acknowledged (smaller gains on insertion, models trained only on discrimination). Reproducibility is partially supported (hyperparameters reported) but code is not mentioned and exclusion of some feature types may hinder exact replication.

main_claims: Proposes a neural local coherence model that neuralizes the entity grid representation using a convolutional architecture.
The model captures long-range entity transitions and can incorporate entity-specific features via distributed representations and convolutional feature learning.
Introduces a pairwise ranking training method to learn task-specific high-level features in an end-to-end fashion.
Reports state-of-the-art results, outperforming existing models on three coherence assessment tasks (discrimination, insertion, and summary coherence rating).
_summary.methodology: A convolutional neural network operating over the entity grid (including entity-specific feature encodings) models long-range entity transitions; the network is trained end-to-end using a pairwise ranking objective to score and rank candidate document orderings or summaries.
datasets: Wall Street Journal (WSJ) portion of the Penn Treebank
metrics: Summary coherence rating (pairwise ranking vs. human judgments)
limitations: Gains on the insertion task are smaller; insertion is reported as a harder task.
The models were trained on the discrimination task (original vs. random permutation) and may not be optimal for insertion since they were not trained specifically for it.
The Extended Grid-CNN experiments include only a subset (three) of the Extended Grid entity-specific features and exclude features requiring external corpora.
